---
title: "Mathematical Imaging"
subtitle: "Online book for MATH 435"
---

This online book is the narrative companion to the MATH 435 slides and notebooks.
The slides are designed for class structure; the book is designed for slower reading, review, and project preparation.

The [motivation chapter](motivation.qmd) develops the central identity of the course:

$$
y = Ax+\eta
\qquad\Longrightarrow\qquad
\hat{x}
=
\arg\min_x D(Ax,y)+\lambda R(x)
\qquad\Longrightarrow\qquad
\hat{x}=f_\theta(y).
$$

Mathematical imaging begins with inverse problems and naturally leads to neural networks as learned inverse maps, learned priors, learned denoisers, or learned components of reconstruction algorithms. The book keeps these ideas in one story: a reconstruction must be explained by the data, the forward model, the assumptions, and the evidence that it can be trusted.

The formula above should be read as the spine of the book, not as three separate topics. The left side describes how measurements are formed. The middle describes how classical reconstruction balances data fit and prior assumptions. The right side describes one modern possibility: a learned map or learned component. The course keeps asking when these arrows are justified.

The book contains the motivation chapter and the fourteen teaching chapters. It is now the main long-form reference for the course arc. The final project, report, and oral defense are handled separately on the [project page](../project/index.qmd).

Each chapter connects:

- the mathematical model;
- the computational idea;
- the relevant figures;
- links to the slide deck;
- links to the runnable notebook.

## How To Use This Book

Read each chapter before or after the corresponding week. Use the slides for the two-session lecture structure, the notebook for experiments, and the book for the slower explanation that connects the mathematics to the course arc. The [preparation rhythm](../preparation.qmd) gives the weekly sequence that connects these resources.

For each chapter, students should be able to answer:

1. What is the mathematical object?
2. What is the forward model?
3. What information is lost or degraded?
4. What computation tests the idea?
5. What prior, regularizer, or learned information is being used?
6. What can go wrong?

The same six questions apply to classical and neural methods. The notation changes, but the responsibility does not.

## Reading Threads

Four threads run through the whole book.

| Thread | Guiding question |
|---|---|
| measurement | What did the data actually record? |
| prior | What assumptions choose among possible reconstructions? |
| computation | What algorithm, iteration, or learned map produces the image? |
| evidence | What tests justify trusting the output? |

The chapters should not be read as isolated techniques. Fourier analysis, Tikhonov regularization, TV, sparsity, wavelets, and learned denoisers are different answers to the same reconstruction problem. Each answer changes what information is emphasized, what information is assumed, and what evidence must be reported.

## Current Chapters

| Chapter | Topic | Slides | Notebook |
|---:|---|---|---|
| - | [Motivation: from inverse problems to neural networks](motivation.qmd) | - | - |
| 1 | [Images as mathematical objects](week-01-image-formation.qmd) | [slides](../slides/week-01-image-formation.html) | [notebook](../notebooks/week01_image_formation.ipynb) / [Colab](https://colab.research.google.com/github/lnajman/math435-mathematical-imaging/blob/main/notebooks/week01_image_formation.ipynb) |
| 2 | [Convolution and blur](week-02-convolution-blur.qmd) | [slides](../slides/week-02-convolution-blur.html) | [notebook](../notebooks/week02_convolution_blur.ipynb) / [Colab](https://colab.research.google.com/github/lnajman/math435-mathematical-imaging/blob/main/notebooks/week02_convolution_blur.ipynb) |
| 3 | [Fourier transform in imaging](week-03-fourier-imaging.qmd) | [slides](../slides/week-03-fourier-imaging.html) | [notebook](../notebooks/week03_fourier_imaging.ipynb) / [Colab](https://colab.research.google.com/github/lnajman/math435-mathematical-imaging/blob/main/notebooks/week03_fourier_imaging.ipynb) |
| 4 | [Noise models and likelihoods](week-04-noise-likelihood.qmd) | [slides](../slides/week-04-noise-likelihood.html) | [notebook](../notebooks/week04_noise_likelihood.ipynb) / [Colab](https://colab.research.google.com/github/lnajman/math435-mathematical-imaging/blob/main/notebooks/week04_noise_likelihood.ipynb) |
| 5 | [Ill-posed inverse problems](week-05-ill-posed-inverse-problems.qmd) | [slides](../slides/week-05-ill-posed-inverse-problems.html) | [notebook](../notebooks/week05_ill_posed_inverse_problems.ipynb) / [Colab](https://colab.research.google.com/github/lnajman/math435-mathematical-imaging/blob/main/notebooks/week05_ill_posed_inverse_problems.ipynb) |
| 6 | [Tikhonov regularization](week-06-tikhonov-regularization.qmd) | [slides](../slides/week-06-tikhonov-regularization.html) | [notebook](../notebooks/week06_tikhonov_regularization.ipynb) / [Colab](https://colab.research.google.com/github/lnajman/math435-mathematical-imaging/blob/main/notebooks/week06_tikhonov_regularization.ipynb) |
| 7 | [Variational formulation](week-07-variational-formulation.qmd) | [slides](../slides/week-07-variational-formulation.html) | [notebook](../notebooks/week07_variational_formulation.ipynb) / [Colab](https://colab.research.google.com/github/lnajman/math435-mathematical-imaging/blob/main/notebooks/week07_variational_formulation.ipynb) |
| 8 | [Total Variation regularization](week-08-total-variation.qmd) | [slides](../slides/week-08-total-variation.html) | [notebook](../notebooks/week08_total_variation.ipynb) / [Colab](https://colab.research.google.com/github/lnajman/math435-mathematical-imaging/blob/main/notebooks/week08_total_variation.ipynb) |
| 9 | [Optimization methods](week-09-optimization-methods.qmd) | [slides](../slides/week-09-optimization-methods.html) | [notebook](../notebooks/week09_optimization_methods.ipynb) / [Colab](https://colab.research.google.com/github/lnajman/math435-mathematical-imaging/blob/main/notebooks/week09_optimization_methods.ipynb) |
| 10 | [Sparse reconstruction](week-10-sparse-reconstruction.qmd) | [slides](../slides/week-10-sparse-reconstruction.html) | [notebook](../notebooks/week10_sparse_reconstruction.ipynb) / [Colab](https://colab.research.google.com/github/lnajman/math435-mathematical-imaging/blob/main/notebooks/week10_sparse_reconstruction.ipynb) |
| 11 | [Wavelets and multiscale representation](week-11-wavelets.qmd) | [slides](../slides/week-11-wavelets.html) | [notebook](../notebooks/week11_wavelets.ipynb) / [Colab](https://colab.research.google.com/github/lnajman/math435-mathematical-imaging/blob/main/notebooks/week11_wavelets.ipynb) |
| 12 | [Model-based vs data-driven imaging](week-12-model-data.qmd) | [slides](../slides/week-12-model-data.html) | [notebook](../notebooks/week12_model_data.ipynb) / [Colab](https://colab.research.google.com/github/lnajman/math435-mathematical-imaging/blob/main/notebooks/week12_model_data.ipynb) |
| 13 | [Plug-and-play and learned regularization](week-13-plug-and-play.qmd) | [slides](../slides/week-13-plug-and-play.html) | [notebook](../notebooks/week13_plug_and_play.ipynb) / [Colab](https://colab.research.google.com/github/lnajman/math435-mathematical-imaging/blob/main/notebooks/week13_plug_and_play.ipynb) |
| 14 | [Stability, robustness, and ethics](week-14-stability-robustness-ethics.qmd) | [slides](../slides/week-14-stability-robustness-ethics.html) | [notebook](../notebooks/week14_stability_robustness.ipynb) / [Colab](https://colab.research.google.com/github/lnajman/math435-mathematical-imaging/blob/main/notebooks/week14_stability_robustness.ipynb) |

## Book Arc

The book follows a single course arc:

| Part | Chapters | Theme |
|---|---|---|
| Start | Motivation | Inverse problems as the path toward neural reconstruction |
| I | 1-3 | Images, operators, convolution, Fourier analysis |
| II | 4-6 | Noise, likelihoods, ill-posedness, Tikhonov regularization |
| III | 7-9 | Variational models, TV, and optimization |
| IV | 10-13 | Sparsity, wavelets, learned priors, plug-and-play methods |
| V | 14 | Robustness, ethics, and responsible reconstruction |

The course project extends this arc by asking students to build and defend their own reconstruction study. It is part of the course, but it is not a separate book chapter.

## How The Parts Connect

The early chapters define images and measurements. Chapters 4-6 explain why measurement agreement alone is not enough: noise, missing information, and instability force us to add assumptions. Chapters 7-9 turn those assumptions into variational models and algorithms. Chapters 10-11 ask where image information is sparse. Chapters 12-13 ask whether those priors, denoisers, or update rules can be learned from examples. Chapter 14 returns to trust: what was measured, what was assumed, what was learned, and what evidence supports the reconstruction?

This is why the slides and book have different jobs. The slides give the live class path. The book gives the slower argument connecting the weeks.

## Notation

We will use:

- $u$ for a continuous image function;
- $U$ for a discrete image array;
- $x=\operatorname{vec}(U)$ for a vectorized image;
- $A$ for a forward operator;
- $y$ for measured data;
- $\eta$ for noise;
- $\hat{x}$ for a reconstruction.

The meaning of $A$ changes from chapter to chapter: it may be a mask, a blur, a Fourier sampling operator, or a more general measurement model.
